works: names the 2026 frame the reader already has
pivots immediately to the money question, which is what an investor cares about
weaker: the phrasing 'go to work' is a cliche the reader has heard 50 times
no specific number, so it reads as opinion not signal
buries the contrarian edge (the money is NOT where the demos are)
current_analysis.emotional_impact: mild recognition; should be sharper FOMO + a contrarian jolt for a skeptical investor

candidates.text: The foundation-model wars are over, and the winners are about to be commoditized. The next fortune in enterprise AI will be made one layer up — by whoever makes agents reliable enough to trust with real money.
candidates.why_it_works: leads with a contrarian claim that flatters the investor's skepticism and frames the entire thesis (value migrates up the stack) in two sentences

candidates.text: Last quarter I sat through a dozen agent demos that all worked flawlessly. Then I asked each founder the same question — 'what's your task-completion rate in production?' — and watched the room go quiet. That silence is the whole investment thesis.
candidates.why_it_works: dramatizes the demo-vs-reliability gap with a first-person scene; ends on the exact metric the piece tells investors to demand

candidates.text: Enterprises spent $13.8 billion on generative AI last year — roughly six times the year before — and almost none of the durable margin will end up with the companies that built the models. Here's where it goes instead.
candidates.why_it_works: opens with a hard, sourced number (credibility for a skeptical reader), then immediately subverts the obvious conclusion, creating curiosity and promising a payoff

recommendation_rationale: For a VC audience the sourced $13.8B figure buys instant credibility and the 'almost none will go to the model-builders' twist delivers the contrarian thesis in one line. The personal-story option is strong and kept as a back-pocket alternative if the writer wants a warmer open.
source: content-research-writer step 4 — Improve Hooks (current-hook analysis + three alternatives: bold statement / personal story / surprising data, each with why-it-works + hook checks)
